analysis.phrase: than would be used in writing

sentences.translation: 이것은 화자들이 자신의 요점에 대해 더 오래 말하는 것을 필요하게 만든다, / 글쓰기에서 같은 생각을 표현하는 데 사용될 것보다 / 더 많은 단어를 사용하면서.

blank_inference: 말하기는 글쓰기보다 더 많은 단어가 필요하다는 대조
청자는 다시 들을 수 없다는 말하기의 제약
청자가 쉽게 산만해진다는 특성
화자는 반복과 정교화를 통해 보완해야 한다는 결론
transformations.mood_change_desc:
이 글은 객관적 설명문으로 심경 변화는 없으나,
글쓰기의 간결성 원칙 ↔ 말하기의 반복·정교화 필요성이라는
명확한 대조 구조가 핵심이다.
grammar_points: 비교급 구문: 'It takes more words to ~ than it takes in writing' (말하기와 글쓰기 대조)
대조 접속사 While: 'While the reader can reread, the listener cannot rehear' (독자 vs 청자)
가목적어 it: 'This makes it necessary for speakers to talk longer' (진목적어는 to 부정사)
분사구문: 'using more words on them than would be used ~' (동시 동작·수단)
transformations.title_theme_options: 제목으로 '말하기는 왜 더 많은 단어가 필요한가' 또는 '말하기와 글쓰기의 차이'가 적절하다. 주제는 '청자는 다시 들을 수 없고 산만하므로, 화자는 글보다 더 많은 단어로 반복·정교화해야 한다'가 정답이다.

data.background: 말하기와 글쓰기의 차이는 의사소통 연구의 핵심 주제다. 글은 독자가 재독 가능하지만, 말은 재생 불가능하고 청자의 주의 산만 위험이 크므로 화자는 반복과 정교화로 보완해야 한다.

data.passage:
Experts on writing say, "Get rid of as many words as possible." Each word must do something important. If it
doesn't, get rid of it. Well, this doesn't work for speaking. It takes more words to introduce, express, and
adequately elaborate an idea in speech than it takes in writing. Why is this so? While the reader can reread, the
listener cannot rehear. Speakers do not come equipped with a replay button. Because listeners are easily
distracted, they will miss many pieces of what a speaker says. If they miss the crucial sentence, they may never
catch up. This makes it necessary for speakers to talk longer about their points, using more words on them than
would be used to express the same idea in writing.

data.topic_en: Making Meetings More Productive
data.topic_ko: 회의는 창의적 사고를 장려하지만 평균적으로 1/3의 시간이 비생산적이다. 사전 준비와 안건 공유를 통해 회의를 더 생산적으로 만들 수 있다.
data.expected_title: How to Make Meetings More Productive
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 문제 제시 후 해결책 제안
data.mood_change: 중립·문제 인식 (consider unproductive) → 긍정·해결 제시 (more productive and useful)
data.passage_html: Meetings encourage creative thinking and can give you ideas that you may never have thought of on your own. However, on average, meeting participants consider about one third of meeting time to be unproductive. But you can make your meetings more productive and more useful by preparing well in advance. You should create a list of items to be discussed and share your list with other participants before a meeting. It allows them to know what to expect in your meeting and prepare to participate.
data.topic_sentence_en: You can make your meetings more productive and more useful by preparing well in advance.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 사전에 철저히 준비함으로써 회의를 더 생산적이고 유용하게 만들 수 있다. 문제 해결의 핵심 방법을 제시하는 중심문이다.

sentences.sentence_html: But you can make your meetings more productive and more useful by preparing well in advance.
sentences.translation: 하지만 당신은 회의를 더 생산적이고 유용하게 만들 수 있다 / 사전에 철저히 준비함으로써

sentences.pattern_name: 구체적 방법 1 (should + 동사원형 / to be p.p.)
sentences.sentence_html: You should create a list of items to be discussed and share your list with other participants before a meeting.
sentences.translation: 당신은 논의될 항목 목록을 만들어야 하고 / 회의 전에 다른 참가자들과 목록을 공유해야 한다

sentences.sentence_html: It allows them to know what to expect in your meeting and prepare to participate.
sentences.translation: 그것은 그들이 회의에서 무엇을 기대할지 알도록 해주고 / 참여할 준비를 하게 한다

blank_inference: 문맥상 '회의를 더 생산적으로 만드는 방법'이 핵심이므로 빈칸에는 'preparing well in advance' 또는 'sharing the agenda beforehand' 유형의 답이 적절
However 이후 문제 제시, But 이후 해결책 제시 구조 파악 필요
allow + 목적어 + to부정사 구문이 '효과·결과'를 나타냄
transformations.mood_change_desc:
문제 인식(비생산적 회의) → 해결 제시(사전 준비)로 흐름 전환
긍정적 효과 강조(creative thinking) → 부정적 현실(unproductive) → 긍정적 해결책(more productive)의 3단 구조
grammar_points: encourage + 목적어: 동사원형이 목적어로 오는 타동사
consider + 목적어 + to be + 보어: 5형식 구문, 'A를 B로 여기다'
make + 목적어 + 형용사: 5형식, '목적어를 ~하게 만들다'
allow + 목적어 + to부정사: 5형식, '목적어가 ~하도록 허락하다/가능하게 하다'
transformations.title_theme_options:
제목: '효율적 회의 준비의 중요성' 또는 '생산적인 회의 만들기'
주제: 사전 준비와 안건 공유를 통한 회의 생산성 향상

data.background: 효과적인 회의 운영은 사전 준비(preparing in advance)와 명확한 안건 공유(sharing agenda)가 핵심이다. 연구에 따르면 회의 시간의 상당 부분이 비생산적으로 소비되므로, 체계적 준비가 필수적이다.

data.passage:
Meetings encourage creative thinking and can give you ideas that you may never have thought of on your own.
However, on average, meeting participants consider about one third of meeting time to be unproductive. But
you can make your meetings more productive and more useful by preparing well in advance. You should create
a list of items to be discussed and share your list with other participants before a meeting. It allows them to
know what to expect in your meeting and prepare to participate.

data.topic_en: Active Listening in Mentoring
data.topic_ko: 멘토링에서 가장 중요한 것은 말하기가 아니라 경청이다. 멘티 스스로 생각하고 말할 수 있도록 귀 기울여 듣는 것이 진정한 멘토의 역할이다.
data.expected_title: The Power of Listening: A Mentor's Key Role
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 멘토링 방법론 제시 / 경청의 중요성 강조
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (논리적 설득 중심)
data.passage_html: It has been said that most people listen with the intention to reply rather than to understand. Your job as a mentor is to facilitate your mentee's thinking and not to try and do it for them, no matter how tempting that may be. If, during a mentoring session, you realise you're doing most of the talking, then I'd respectfully suggest that you just stop, sit back and listen. A good part of the mentee's learning process happens when he or she thinks out loud. The rest of it happens by reflection and practice outside mentoring sessions. Therefore, your mentee should be doing most of the talking. Listening actively and empathically helps mentees to gain insight and to express themselves more effectively. For your mentee to have a sense of being heard and of feeling acknowledged, it is essential that you listen well.
data.topic_sentence_en: For your mentee to have a sense of being heard and of feeling acknowledged, it is essential that you listen well.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 멘티가 자신의 말을 들어주고 인정받는다는 느낌을 받으려면, 당신이 잘 듣는 것이 필수적이다. (가주어 it - 진주어 that 구문으로 '경청'의 본질적 중요성 강조)

sentences.pattern_name: It has been said that 구문 (무생물 주어)
sentences.sentence_html: It has been said that most people listen with the intention to reply rather than to understand.

sentences.sentence_html: Your job as a mentor is to facilitate your mentee's thinking and not to try and do it for them, no matter how tempting that may be.
sentences.translation: 멘토로서 당신의 역할은 / 멘티의 사고를 촉진하는 것이며 / 그들을 대신해 해주려 하지 않는 것이다 / 아무리 그렇게 하고 싶어도

sentences.sentence_html: If, during a mentoring session, you realise you're doing most of the talking, then I'd respectfully suggest that you just stop, sit back and listen.
sentences.translation: 만약 멘토링 세션 중에 / 당신이 대부분 말하고 있다는 것을 깨닫는다면 / 그냥 멈추고 뒤로 물러나 / 경청하라고 정중히 제안하고 싶다

sentences.sentence_html: A good part of the mentee's learning process happens when he or she thinks out loud. The rest of it happens by reflection and practice outside mentoring sessions.

analysis.phrase: when he or she thinks out loud

sentences.translation: 멘티 학습 과정의 상당 부분은 / 그가 소리 내어 생각할 때 일어난다 / 나머지는 성찰과 실천을 통해 / 멘토링 세션 밖에서 일어난다

sentences.sentence_html: Therefore, your mentee should be doing most of the talking. Listening actively and empathically helps mentees to gain insight and to express themselves more effectively.
sentences.translation: 따라서 멘티가 대부분 말해야 한다 / 적극적이고 공감적으로 듣는 것은 / 멘티가 통찰을 얻고 / 자신을 더 효과적으로 표현하도록 돕는다

sentences.sentence_html: For your mentee to have a sense of being heard and of feeling acknowledged, it is essential that you listen well.
sentences.translation: 멘티가 자신의 말을 들어주고 / 인정받는다는 느낌을 갖기 위해서는 / 당신이 잘 듣는 것이 필수적이다

blank_inference: 멘토는 멘티의 사고를 '촉진(facilitate)'해야 하며, 대신 해주려 하지 말아야 한다
멘티 학습의 상당 부분은 '소리 내어 생각할 때(thinks out loud)' 일어난다
적극적·공감적 경청이 멘티의 '통찰(insight)'과 표현력을 높인다
멘티가 '인정받는다는 느낌(feeling acknowledged)'을 갖도록 잘 듣는 것이 필수적이다
transformations.mood_change_desc:
이 글은 심경 변화가 아니라 논리적 설득 구조.
문제 제기(①) → 멘토 역할 정의(②) → 실천 제안(③) → 학습 원리(④⑤) → 결론(⑥)
grammar_points: 가주어 it - 진주어 that 구문: 'it is essential that you listen well'
suggest that 주어 (should) 동사원형: 제안·당위 표현
to부정사의 명사적 용법: 'Your job is to facilitate'
no matter how 양보절: '아무리 ~해도'
transformations.title_theme_options:
제목: '경청의 힘' / '멘토의 진짜 역할' / '말하기보다 듣기'
주제: 멘토링에서 경청의 중요성 / 멘티 중심 대화의 필요성

data.background: 멘토링(mentoring)은 경험 많은 사람이 후배를 지도하는 과정으로, 경청(active listening)과 공감(empathy)이 핵심이다. 멘티 스스로 사고하고 표현하도록 돕는 것이 진정한 멘토의 역할이다.

data.passage:
원장님, 이 지문은 멘토링의 핵심인 '경청'을 다루고 있네요. 특히 마지막 문장은 GTS(가주어-진주어) 구문을 활용한 영작/어순 배열 문제로 내기에 아주 좋은 포인트입니다.

구문분석학습기에 바로 업로드하실 수 있도록 어순을 정답으로 조합하여 깔끔하게 정리해 드립니다.

## 01. 구문분석학습기 업로드용 텍스트
It has been said that most people listen with the intention to reply rather than to understand. Your job as a mentor is to facilitate your mentee's thinking and not to try and do it for them, no matter how tempting that may be. If, during a mentoring session, you realise you're doing most of the talking, then I'd respectfully suggest that you just stop, sit back and listen. A good part of the mentee's learning process happens when he or she thinks out loud. The rest of it happens by reflection and practice outside mentoring sessions. Therefore, your mentee should be doing most of the talking. Listening actively and empathically helps mentees to gain insight and to express themselves more effectively. For your mentee to have a sense of being heard and of feeling acknowledged, it is essential that you listen well.

data.topic_en: Topic: A traveler's devastation after realizing she left her passport at home
data.topic_ko: Maya는 1년 동안 기다려온 유럽 배낭여행을 앞두고 공항에서 탑승 수속을 하던 중 여권을 집에 두고 온 것을 깨달았다. 꿈꿔왔던 여행이 무산되며 깊은 절망감에 빠진 그녀의 심경을 다룬다.
data.expected_title: The Heartbreak of a Forgotten Passport
data.text_type: 서사문 (narrative) — 시간 순서 / 인물의 심리 변화 중심
data.mood_change: 기대·설렘 (expectations were really high) → 절망·상심 (heartbroken)
data.passage_html: Maya waited in line to check in for her flight. Her expectations about her European backpacking trip were really high. She had been looking forward to the trip for a year. She couldn't wait to visit museums in Madrid and see the Eiffel Tower at night in Paris. As she stood in line, she could feel those experiences were finally so close. When she approached the counter, the airline employee asked to see her passport. Maya reached into her pocket but felt nothing. She realized she had left her passport at home. Her plans were ruined. She was heartbroken, knowing she could not board the flight and had to delay her dream trip.
data.topic_sentence_en: She was heartbroken, knowing she could not board the flight and had to delay her dream trip.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 그녀는 비행기에 탑승할 수 없고 꿈꿔 왔던 여행을 연기해야 한다는 것을 깨달으며 상심했다. 이 문장은 여권 분실로 인한 절망감이라는 글의 핵심 주제를 명확히 드러낸다.

sentences.sentence_html: Maya waited in line to check in for her flight. Her expectations about her European backpacking trip were really high.
sentences.translation: Maya는 비행기 탑승 수속을 위해 줄을 서서 기다렸다. / 유럽 배낭여행에 대한 그녀의 기대는 아주 높았다.

sentences.sentence_html: She had been looking forward to the trip for a year. She couldn't wait to visit museums in Madrid and see the Eiffel Tower at night in Paris.
sentences.translation: 그녀는 일 년 동안 이 여행을 손꼽아 기다려 왔다. / 그녀는 빨리 마드리드의 박물관들을 방문하고 파리에서 밤에 에펠 탑을 보고 싶었다.

sentences.sentence_html: As she stood in line, she could feel those experiences were finally so close.
sentences.translation: 줄을 서 있는 동안, 그녀는 그 경험들이 마침내 정말 가까워졌다고 느낄 수 있었다.

sentences.sentence_html: When she approached the counter, the airline employee asked to see her passport. Maya reached into her pocket but felt nothing.
sentences.translation: 그녀가 카운터에 다가갔을 때, 항공사 직원이 여권을 보여 달라고 요청했다. / Maya는 주머니에 손을 넣었지만 아무것도 만져지지 않았다.

sentences.sentence_html: She realized she had left her passport at home. Her plans were ruined.
sentences.translation: 그녀는 여권을 집에 두고 온 것을 깨달았다. / 그녀의 계획은 망쳐졌다.

sentences.sentence_html: She was heartbroken, knowing she could not board the flight and had to delay her dream trip.
sentences.translation: 그녀는 비행기에 탑승할 수 없고 꿈꿔 왔던 여행을 연기해야 한다는 것을 깨달으며, 상심했다.

vocabulary.note: break one's heart: 가슴을 아프게 하다

blank_inference: 빈칸: She was _____, knowing she could not board. → heartbroken (절망감)
빈칸: Her _____ were really high. → expectations (기대)
빈칸: She had been _____ the trip for a year. → looking forward to (기대 지속)
빈칸: Maya _____ her pocket but felt nothing. → reached into (행동 묘사)
transformations.mood_change_desc:
초반: 높은 기대와 설렘 (expectations were really high, couldn't wait)
중반: 꿈이 가까워진 듯한 느낌 (finally so close)
전환점: 여권 분실 깨달음 (felt nothing, realized)
후반: 깊은 절망과 상심 (heartbroken, plans were ruined)
grammar_points: 과거완료 진행: 'had been looking forward to' - 과거 시점 이전부터 계속된 기대
지각동사 + 명사절: 'could feel (that) those experiences were close' - that 생략 가능
분사구문 (이유): 'knowing she could not board' - because she knew의 축약
병렬 구조: 'visit museums and see the Eiffel Tower' - to부정사 병렬 연결

심경: 기대와 설렘에서 절망과 상심으로의 급격한 전환

data.background: 해외여행 시 여권은 필수 서류이며, 분실 시 탑승이 불가능하다. 과거완료 진행형은 과거 특정 시점 이전부터 계속된 동작을 나타내며, 분사구문은 이유·동시동작 등을 간결하게 표현한다.

data.topic_en: Delay in Table Tennis Center Reopening Due to Electrical Issues
data.topic_ko: 탁구 센터 관리자가 회원들에게 재개장 연기를 안내하는 공지문입니다. 수리 중 예상치 못한 전기 문제가 발견되어 4월 1일에서 5월 1일로 연기되었습니다.
data.expected_title: Notice of Delayed Reopening: Table Tennis Center
data.text_type: 공지문 (notice) — 상황 설명 및 변경 사항 안내
data.mood_change: 감사·기대 (Thank you for your interest) → 유감·사과 (regret to inform, delayed)
data.passage_html:
Dear Connexa Point Table Tennis Center members,
I am the manager of the Connexa Point Table Tennis Center. Thank you for your interest in the upcoming reopening of the center. Unfortunately, during the repair process, unexpected electrical issues were discovered, causing the work to take longer than we planned. We regret to inform you that the center's reopening must be delayed. The center was originally scheduled to reopen on April 1st. However, it will now reopen on May 1st to ensure the safety of all members. We look forward to seeing you back on the court soon. Thank you for your patience and understanding.
Sincerely,
Manager of the Table Tennis Center
data.topic_sentence_en: We regret to inform you that the center's reopening must be delayed.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 센터의 재개장이 연기되어야 함을 유감스럽게 알려드립니다. — 공지의 핵심 내용을 전달하는 중심 문장입니다.

sentences.sentence_html: I am the manager of the Connexa Point Table Tennis Center. Thank you for your interest in the upcoming reopening of the center.
sentences.translation: 저는 Connexa Point 탁구 센터의 관리자입니다. / 곧 있을 센터 재개장에 관심 가져주셔서 감사합니다.

sentences.sentence_html: Unfortunately, during the repair process, unexpected electrical issues were discovered, causing the work to take longer than we planned.
sentences.translation: 불행히도, 수리 과정 중에 예상치 못한 전기 문제가 발견되었고, / 이로 인해 작업이 계획보다 더 오래 걸리게 되었습니다.

sentences.sentence_html: We regret to inform you that the center's reopening must be delayed.

analysis.explanation: 유감스러운 소식을 전할 때 쓰는 격식체 표현

sentences.translation: 센터의 재개장이 연기되어야 함을 / 유감스럽게 알려드립니다.

sentences.sentence_html: The center was originally scheduled to reopen on April 1st. However, it will now reopen on May 1st to ensure the safety of all members.

analysis.explanation: 앞 문장과 대조를 이루는 역접 연결사
안전을 보장하기 위해, 목적을 나타내는 부정사

sentences.translation: 센터는 원래 4월 1일에 재개장할 예정이었습니다. / 하지만 모든 회원의 안전을 보장하기 위해 5월 1일에 재개장할 것입니다.

sentences.sentence_html: We look forward to seeing you back on the court soon. Thank you for your patience and understanding.
sentences.translation: 곧 코트에서 여러분을 다시 뵙기를 / 기대합니다. 인내와 이해에 감사드립니다.

blank_inference: 연기 사유: 'unexpected electrical issues' — 예상치 못한 전기 문제
관리자의 태도: 'regret to inform', 'thank you for your patience' — 유감과 감사 표현
안전 강조: 'to ensure the safety of all members' — 회원 안전 우선
날짜 변경: originally April 1st → now May 1st
transformations.mood_change_desc:
관리자는 처음 회원들의 관심에 감사하며 기대감을 드러내지만,
예상치 못한 문제 발견으로 유감과 사과의 어조로 전환됩니다.
마지막에는 다시 긍정적 기대와 감사로 마무리합니다.
grammar_points: 'Thank you for + 명사/동명사' 구조 (감사 표현)
'regret to inform you that절' (유감 통지 격식 표현)
'cause + 목적어 + to부정사' (5형식 사역동사)
'look forward to + 동명사' (to는 전치사)
transformations.title_theme_options: '센터 재개장 연기 안내' 또는 '예상치 못한 전기 문제로 인한 일정 변경' — 공지의 핵심은 연기 사실과 사유 전달

data.background: 공식 공지문(notice)은 변경 사항을 명확히 전달하며, 'regret to inform', 'look forward to' 같은 격식체 표현을 사용합니다. 독자의 이해와 협조를 구하는 공손한 어조가 특징입니다.

data.topic_en: Topic: Maintenance Notice for Underground Parking Facility
data.topic_ko: 스포츠 센터가 지하 주차장 필수 보수 공사로 인해 4월 1일부터 7일까지 지하 주차장 이용이 불가함을 안내하고, 임시 외부 주차장 이용을 요청하며 불편에 대해 사과하는 공지문입니다.
data.expected_title: Notice: Underground Parking Maintenance
data.text_type: 안내문 (notice) — 사실 전달 및 협조 요청 중심
data.mood_change: 감사·안내 (appreciate) → 사과·협조 요청 (apologize, appreciate cooperation)
data.passage_html: We greatly appreciate your continued use of our sports center. As previously announced, we will be conducting essential maintenance on our underground parking lot. As a result, the underground parking facility will be unavailable from April 1st to April 7th. During this period, we kindly ask you to use the temporary outdoor parking lot located across from the main entrance. We are committed to completing the work as quickly as possible. We sincerely apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and appreciate your patience and cooperation.

transformations.mood_change_desc:
초반 감사 표현(appreciate)에서 시작해, 보수 공사 안내와 사과(apologize)로 이어지며, 마지막에 협조 요청과 재차 감사(appreciate cooperation)로 마무리하는 정중하고 배려 있는 tone의 공지문입니다.
고객의 불편을 최소화하려는 노력과 협조에 대한 감사가 핵심입니다.
grammar_points: 동명사 목적어: 'appreciate your continued use', 'committed to completing' (전치사 to 뒤 동명사)
수동태: 'will be unavailable', 'will be conducting' (미래 상태·진행 표현)
요청 구문: 'we kindly ask you to use' (ask + 목적어 + to부정사)
분사 수식: 'located across from' (과거분사가 명사 수식)

data.passage:
We greatly appreciate your continued use of our sports center. 
저희 스포츠 센터를 지속적으로 이용해 주셔서 대단히 감사합니다. 

As previously announced, we will be conducting essential maintenance on our underground parking lot. 
사전에 공지된 바와 같이, 저희는 지하 주차장의 필수 보수 공사를 시행하고자 합니다. 

As a result, the underground parking facility will be unavailable from April 1st to April 7th. 
그 결과, 4월 1일부터 4월 7일까지 지하 주차장 시설은 이용 불가합니다. 

During this period, we kindly ask you to use the temporary outdoor parking lot located across from the main entrance. 
이 기간 동안, 정문 맞은편에 위치한 임시 외부 주차장을 이용해 주시기를 정중히 부탁드립니다. 

We are committed to completing the work as quickly as possible. 
가능한 한 빨리 작업을 마무리하는 데 전념하겠습니다. 

We sincerely apologize for any inconvenience this may cause and appreciate your patience and cooperation.
이로 인해 발생할 수 있는 모든 불편에 대해 진심으로 사과드리며 여러분의 인내와 협조에 감사드립니다.

data.topic_en: Topic: A New Student's First Day and an Act of Kindness
data.topic_ko: 전학 온 첫날 구내식당에서 불안감을 느끼던 Oliver가 수학 시간에 펜을 빌려준 학생의 작은 친절로 마음의 벽이 허물어지며 안도감을 느끼게 되는 이야기.
data.expected_title: A Small Kindness That Broke Down the Wall
data.text_type: 서사문 (narrative) — 시간 순서 / 인물의 심리 변화 중심
data.mood_change: 불안·고립감 (felt anxious, invisible wall) → 안도·편안함 (feel at ease, smiled brightly)
data.passage_html: Looking around the noisy cafeteria, Oliver gripped his tray tightly. He felt the heavy weight of being new to the school. Not knowing how the day would go made him feel anxious. He sat with a heavy sigh at a corner table. He thought, 'Can I make it through the rest of the day?' Just then, a plastic tray was placed down opposite him. It was a boy from his math class. 'Hey, is this seat taken? By the way, thanks for lending me that pen earlier.' The invisible wall around Oliver seemed to melt away. 'Not at all, please sit,' he replied. Starting to feel at ease, he smiled brightly at the boy.

transformations.mood_change_desc:

Oliver의 심리 변화는 타인의 작은 배려가 얼마나 큰 영향을 미치는지 보여줌.
grammar_points: 분사구문: 'Looking around', 'Starting to feel' — 동시 동작이나 이유
동명사구 주어: 'Not knowing how the day would go' — 부정 동명사구
사역동사 5형식: 'made him feel anxious' — make+목적어+동사원형
지각동사+to부정사: 'seemed to melt away' — 주관적 인상 표현
transformations.title_theme_options:
제목: '작은 친절이 허문 벽' 또는 '고립에서 연결로'
주제: 전학생의 불안을 해소시킨 작은 친절의 힘 / 타인의 배려가 심리적 장벽을 허무는 순간

data.background: 전학생이나 새로운 환경에 적응하는 과정에서 느끼는 고립감(isolation)과 불안(anxiety)은 청소년기에 흔한 심리 상태이다. 이 지문은 작은 친절(small act of kindness)이 이러한 심리적 장벽을 허물고 소속감(sense of belonging)을 형성하는 과정을 보여준다.

data.passage:
Looking around the noisy cafeteria, Oliver gripped his tray tightly. 
시끄러운 구내식당을 둘러보며, Oliver는 자신의 쟁반을 꽉 쥐었다. 

He felt the heavy weight of being new to the school. 
그는 이 학교에 새로 와 있다는 것에 큰 부담감을 느꼈다. 

Not knowing how the day would go made him feel anxious. 
하루가 어떻게 흘러갈지 모른다는 것이 그를 불안하게 느끼도록 만들었다. 

He sat with a heavy sigh at a corner table. 
그는 무거운 한숨과 함께 구석 테이블에 앉았다. 

He thought, ‘Can I make it through the rest of the day?’ 
‘남은 오늘 하루를 잘 헤쳐나갈 수 있을까?’라고 그는 생각했다. 

Just then, a plastic tray was placed down opposite him. 
바로 그때, 플라스틱 쟁반 하나가 그의 맞은편에 놓였다. 

It was a boy from his math class. 
그와 같은 수학 수업에 있던 소년이었다. 

“Hey, is this seat taken? By the way, thanks for lending me that pen earlier.” 
“이봐, 여기 자리 있어? 그런데, 아까 그 펜을 빌려줘서 고마워.” 

The invisible wall around Oliver seemed to melt away. 
Oliver 주변의 보이지 않는 벽이 녹아 사라지는 것 같았다. 

“Not at all, please sit,” he replied. 
“천만에, 앉아.”라고 그가 대답했다. 

Starting to feel at ease, he smiled brightly at the boy.
긴장이 풀리는 것을 느끼기 시작하며, 그는 소년을 향해 밝게 웃었다.

data.topic_en: Topic: A four-year-old girl's first carnival experience turning from excitement to fear
data.topic_ko: 네 살 소녀 Cassie의 첫 카니발 경험. 설렘과 기쁨으로 시작했지만, 거대한 풍선들을 괴물처럼 느끼며 두려움에 얼어붙어 엄마 뒤로 숨는 심경 변화를 그린다.
data.expected_title: Cassie's First Carnival: From Joy to Fear
data.text_type: 서사문 (narrative) — 시간 순서 / 인물의 심리 변화 중심
data.mood_change: 기대·기쁨 (jump with joy) → 두려움·공포 (heart began to beat fast from fear)
data.passage_html: It was the first carnival for the four­year­old girl, Cassie. She had only heard about it from her mother. She had been imagining colorful balloons and delicious candies for a long time. When she finally arrived at the carnival, the twinkling lights and music made her jump with joy. The magician showed his tricks and she cheered with her family. But suddenly, she saw a lot of huge balloons in the parade coming towards her. She thought they were too big, like monsters. Her heart began to beat fast from fear. Cassie froze and dropped her cotton candy on the ground. She quickly hid behind her mother.
data.topic_sentence_en: Her heart began to beat fast from fear.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 그녀의 심장은 두려움으로 빠르게 뛰기 시작했다. — 기쁨에서 공포로의 심경 전환점을 나타내는 중심 문장.

sentences.sentence_html: It was the first carnival for the four­year­old girl, Cassie. She had only heard about it from her mother. She had been imagining colorful balloons and delicious candies for a long time.
sentences.translation: 네 살 소녀 Cassie에게 / 그것은 첫 카니발이었다. / 그녀는 엄마로부터 그것에 대해 / 들어본 것만 있었다. / 그녀는 오랫동안 / 화려한 풍선들과 맛있는 사탕들을 / 상상해 왔었다.

sentences.sentence_html: When she finally arrived at the carnival, the twinkling lights and music made her jump with joy.
sentences.translation: 그녀가 드디어 카니발에 도착했을 때, / 반짝이는 조명들과 음악이 / 그녀를 기쁨으로 / 뛰게 만들었다.

sentences.sentence_html: But suddenly, she saw a lot of huge balloons in the parade coming towards her.
sentences.translation: 하지만 갑자기, / 그녀는 퍼레이드 속 / 많은 거대한 풍선들이 / 자신을 향해 다가오는 것을 / 보았다.

sentences.sentence_html: She thought they were too big, like monsters. Her heart began to beat fast from fear.
sentences.translation: 그녀는 그것들이 너무 크고, / 괴물 같다고 생각했다. / 그녀의 심장은 / 두려움으로 / 빠르게 뛰기 시작했다.

sentences.sentence_html: Cassie froze and dropped her cotton candy on the ground.
sentences.translation: Cassie는 얼어붙었고 / 그녀의 솜사탕을 / 땅에 떨어뜨렸다.

sentences.sentence_html: She quickly hid behind her mother.
sentences.translation: 그녀는 재빨리 / 엄마 뒤로 숨었다.

blank_inference: 심경 변화의 전환점: 'Her heart began to beat fast from fear' — 기쁨에서 공포로 전환
사역동사 구문: 'made her jump with joy' — 조명과 음악이 그녀를 기쁘게 만듦
지각동사 + 분사: 'saw balloons coming towards her' — 풍선들이 다가오는 것을 목격
직유 표현: 'like monsters' — 풍선을 괴물에 비유한 심리적 두려움
transformations.mood_change_desc:
처음: 'jump with joy' — 반짝이는 조명과 음악에 기뻐 뛰며 즐거워함
전환점: 'saw huge balloons coming' / 'like monsters' — 거대한 풍선들을 괴물처럼 인식
결말: 'heart beat fast from fear' / 'froze and hid' — 두려움에 얼어붙어 엄마 뒤로 숨음
grammar_points: 과거완료진행: 'had been imagining' — 과거 특정 시점 이전부터 계속된 상상
사역동사: 'made her jump' — make + 목적어 + 동사원형 (사역)
지각동사 + 현재분사: 'saw balloons coming' — see + 목적어 + -ing (진행 중인 동작)
begin to V: 'began to beat' — 동작의 시작을 나타내는 표현
transformations.title_theme_options: 제목: '카니발의 기쁨과 공포' 또는 '첫 카니발의 두 얼굴' / 주제: 기대했던 즐거운 축제가 예상치 못한 두려움으로 변한 어린 소녀의 첫 경험

data.background: 카니발(Carnival)은 퍼레이드, 음악, 마술쇼, 거대한 풍선 등으로 구성된 축제이다. 어린아이들에게는 설렘과 동시에 낯선 거대한 대상들이 두려움의 원인이 될 수 있다.

data.passage:
It was the first carnival for the fouryearold girl, Cassie. She had only heard about it from her mother. She
had been imagining colorful balloons and delicious candies for a long time. When she finally arrived at
the carnival, the twinkling lights and music made her jump with joy. The magician showed his tricks and
she cheered with her family. But suddenly, she saw a lot of huge balloons in the parade coming towards
her. She thought they were too big, like monsters. Her heart began to beat fast from fear. Cassie froze
and dropped her cotton candy on the ground. She quickly hid behind her mother.

data.topic_en: Building Networks During Transitions Rather Than Staying Narrowly Focused
data.topic_ko: 새로운 상황에 적응할 때 너무 집중만 하면 스트레스에 취약해진다. 대신 전환기를 새로운 네트워크 구축의 발판으로 활용해야 한다.
data.expected_title: Use Transitions as a Jumping-off Point for Building New Networks
data.text_type: 논설문 (argumentative) — 문제 제기 후 해결책 제시
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (논리적 설득 중심)
data.passage_html: When moving into a new situation, many people decide that they need to master what's new. To this end, they reduce their connections to people or activities that might distract them from their goal. But that is a mistake. Being too focused can lead to becoming unidimensional — you have few remedies to the microstresses that are inevitably bombarding you at that time. So when things are tough during such a transition, you have almost nothing else in your life to counterbalance the stress. Instead of staying laser-focused, use this transition as the jumping-off point for building new networks to shape the work you want to do and whom you want to do it with.
data.topic_sentence_en: Instead of staying laser-focused, use this transition as the jumping-off point for building new networks to shape the work you want to do and whom you want to do it with.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 레이저처럼 집중만 하는 대신, 이 전환기를 자신이 하고 싶은 일과 그 일을 누구와 하고 싶은지를 구상하기 위해 새로운 네트워크를 구축하는 발판으로 활용하라. (문제의 해결책 제시)

data.passage:
When moving into a new situation, many people decide that they need to master what’s new. 
새로운 상황으로 들어설 때, 많은 사람들은 새로운 것을 숙지해야겠다고 결심한다. 

To this end, they reduce their connections to people or activities that might distract them from their goal. 
이러한 목적을 위해, 그들은 자신의 목표에서 주의를 분산시킬 수 있는 사람이나 활동과의 연결을 줄인다. 

But that is a mistake. 
하지만 이는 실수다. 

Being too focused can lead to becoming unidimensional — you have few remedies to the microstresses that are inevitably bombarding you at that time. 
너무 집중만 하는 것은 (당신을) 일차원적이 되도록 이끌 수 있으며, 당신에게 그 시기에 불가피하게 쏟아 부어지는 아주 작은 스트레스에 대한 치료제가 거의 없게 된다. 

So when things are tough during such a transition, you have almost nothing else in your life to counterbalance the stress. 
그래서 이러한 전환기에 상황이 힘들어지면, 그 스트레스들을 상쇄할 만한 것이 당신의 삶에는 거의 없게 된다. 

Instead of staying laser-focused, use this transition as the jumping‑off point for building new networks to shape the work you want to do and whom you want to do it with.
레이저처럼 집중만 하는 대신, 이 전환기를 자신이 하고 싶은 일과 그 일을 누구와 하고 싶은지를 구상하기 위해 새로운 네트워크를 구축하는 발판으로 활용하라.

data.topic_en: Topic: The Power of Personal Stories in Persuasion
data.topic_ko: 청중을 설득하고 유대감을 형성하는 가장 효과적인 방법은 개인적인 이야기나 일화를 공유하는 것이다. 논리만으로는 청중과 유대감을 형성하기 어렵지만, 자신의 경험이나 약점을 드러내면 청중이 당신과 동질감을 느끼고 설득에 성공할 수 있다.
data.expected_title: Building Connection Through Personal Storytelling
data.text_type: 설득적 논설문 (persuasive essay) — 주장 제시 및 논거 전개 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (논설문으로 심경 변화 구조 아님)
data.passage_html: There is simply no better way to influence or stir an audience — instantly, powerfully, authentically — than by opening up to them with a personal story or anecdote. To be clear: I'm not saying you need to tell them long stories about your family vacations or show them baby pictures from the stage. I'm saying that you can share a key biographical detail, or an emotion that you're feeling in the moment, or a self-deprecating joke. It is a tried-and-tested way of bonding with an audience of strangers — and of laying the groundwork for you to then persuade them. The harsh reality is that people won't bond with your arguments in a vacuum, but they will, says speech coach Bas van den Beld, 'bond with you' — the person making those arguments. By sharing a revealing story or a personal flaw, you allow audience members a way to identify with you.
data.topic_sentence_en: There is simply no better way to influence or stir an audience than by opening up to them with a personal story or anecdote.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 청중에게 영향을 주거나 감동을 주는 데 개인적인 이야기나 일화로 마음을 여는 것보다 더 나은 방법은 없다. 논리적 주장보다 개인적 경험 공유가 설득의 핵심임을 강조하는 중심 문장이다.

sentences.sentence_html: There is simply no better way to influence or stir an audience — instantly, powerfully, authentically — than by opening up to them with a personal story or anecdote.

sentences.sentence_html: To be clear: I'm not saying you need to tell them long stories about your family vacations or show them baby pictures from the stage. I'm saying that you can share a key biographical detail, or an emotion that you're feeling in the moment, or a self-deprecating joke.

sentences.sentence_html: It is a tried-and-tested way of bonding with an audience of strangers — and of laying the groundwork for you to then persuade them.

sentences.sentence_html: The harsh reality is that people won't bond with your arguments in a vacuum, but they will, says speech coach Bas van den Beld, 'bond with you' — the person making those arguments.
sentences.translation: 가혹한 현실은 / 사람들이 공허한 논리와는 유대감을 형성하지 않지만 / 스피치 코치 Bas van den Beld가 말하듯 / 그 논리를 제시하는 '당신'과는 유대감을 형성한다는 것이다

sentences.sentence_html: By sharing a revealing story or a personal flaw, you allow audience members a way to identify with you.

diagram_nodes.detail_en_html: Personal stories → bonding → identification → effective persuasion
diagram_nodes.detail_ko_html:

data.passage:
There is simply no better way to influence or stir an audience — instantly, powerfully, authentically —
than by opening up to them with a personal story or anecdote. To be clear: I’m not saying you need to
tell them long stories about your family vacations or show them baby pictures from the stage. I’m saying
that you can share a key biographical detail, or an emotion that you’re feeling in the moment, or a selfdeprecating joke. It is a triedandtested way of bonding with an audience of strangers — and of laying the
groundwork for you to then persuade them. The harsh reality is that people won’t bond with your
arguments in a vacuum, but they will, says speech coach Bas van den Beld, “bond with you” — the person
making those arguments. By sharing a revealing story or a personal flaw, you allow audience members a
way to identify with you.

data.topic_en: Topic: Responsibility to future generations and long-term thinking
data.topic_ko: 인류는 유례없는 유전적·문화적·환경적 힘을 가진 시점에 있으며, 오늘날의 결정이 미래 세대에 미칠 영향을 고려해 '좋은 조상'이 되어야 한다. 이로쿼이족의 '7세대 관리' 원칙처럼, 우리는 먼 미래를 내다보며 후손에게 그늘을 빼앗지 말아야 한다.
data.expected_title: Becoming Good Ancestors: Our Responsibility to Future Generations
data.text_type: 논설문 (argumentative) — 현재 상황 제시 후 책임과 실천 방안 제안
data.mood_change: 현재 인식 (unprecedented power) → 책임 자각 (good ancestors) → 실천 다짐 (must not steal)
data.passage_html: We are now at a point of unprecedented genetic, cultural, and environmental power as a species, and we are linked to nearly every other person on Earth. We are embodied individuals trapped within limited time, but we are also networked data streams, memories, and influencers, and part of a grander humanity. Our decisions today have far-reaching impacts that place a responsibility on us to become good ancestors, to take the long view and time travel forward to imagine the wellbeing of billions of people whose lives will be lived in the world we are currently making. Centuries ago, leaders of the native North American Iroquois people created 'seven generation stewardship,' instructing people to consider the impact of every decision on their children, seven generations into the future. In the precious few decades that Earth is ours, while we enjoy the gardens planted by our ancestors, we must not steal the shade from our descendants.
data.topic_sentence_en: Our decisions today have far-reaching impacts that place a responsibility on us to become good ancestors, to take the long view and time travel forward to imagine the wellbeing of billions of people whose lives will be lived in the world we are currently making.

data.passage:
We are now at a point of unprecedented genetic, cultural, and environmental power as a species, and we
are linked to nearly every other person on Earth. We are embodied individuals trapped within limited
time, but we are also networked data streams, memories, and influencers, and part of a grander humanity.
Our decisions today have farreaching impacts that place a responsibility on us to become good ancestors,
to take the long view and time travel forward to imagine the wellbeing of billions of people whose lives
will be lived in the world we are currently making. Centuries ago, leaders of the native North American
Iroquois people created “seven generation stewardship,” instructing people to consider the impact of
every decision on their children, seven generations into the future. In the precious few decades that Earth
is ours, while we enjoy the gardens planted by our ancestors, we must not steal the shade from our
descendants.

data.topic_en: Separation of Commuter and Intercity Traffic in Railway Stations
data.topic_ko: 철도역에서는 통근객과 도시 간 여행자의 동선이 다르므로, 충돌과 혼란을 피하기 위해 별도의 승강장과 경로로 분리해야 한다.
data.expected_title: Separating Commuter and Intercity Passenger Flows
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 문제 제시 및 해결책 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (중립적 설명)
data.passage_html: The usual intercity traveler moves slowly through the station area. The passenger may not be familiar with the routine, have baggage to handle and check or retrieve, have a long wait for connections or delayed trains, and may require information, food, and a comfortable place to sit. Commuters, on the other hand, are familiar with the route through the station, have little or no luggage, and are usually in a hurry. They want direct access to or from local streets and transport. These two types of traffic should be kept separate to avoid conflict and confusion. In some large stations such as Grand Central Terminal in New York City, commuter and intercity trains arrive and depart on different levels. In smaller stations, separate platforms should be used and traffic routed so that the two lines of movement do not cross. In some instances, separate stations are in use. Clear and concise direction and routing signs and other means of channelization are desirable.
data.topic_sentence_en: These two types of traffic should be kept separate to avoid conflict and confusion.

data.passage:
The usual intercity traveler moves slowly through the station area. 
보통의 도시 사이를 이동하는 사람은 역 공간을 천천히 이동해 다닌다. 

The passenger may not be familiar with the routine, have baggage to handle and check or retrieve, have a long wait for connections or delayed trains, and may require information, food, and a comfortable place to sit. 
그 승객은 절차에 익숙하지 않을 수도 있고, 처리하고 맡기거나 되찾아야 할 짐이 있을 수도 있으며, 환승이나 지연된 열차로 인해 오래 기다려야 할 수도 있고, 정보, 음식, 그리고 편안하게 앉을 자리를 필요로 할 수도 있다. 

Commuters, on the other hand, are familiar with the route through the station, have little or no luggage, and are usually in a hurry. 
반면에, 통근객은 역을 통과하는 경로에 익숙하고, 짐이 거의 없거나 전혀 없으며, 대개 서두른다. 

They want direct access to or from local streets and transport. 
그들은 지역 도로와 교통수단으로 오가는 직접적인 접근성을 원한다. 

These two types of traffic should be kept separate to avoid conflict and confusion. 
이러한 두 종류의 교통 (이동)은 충돌과 혼란을 피하기 위해 분리되어 있어야 한다. 

In some large stations such as Grand Central Terminal in New York City, commuter and intercity trains arrive and depart on different levels. 
뉴욕시의 Grand Central Terminal과 같은 몇몇의 대형 역에서는, 통근 열차와 도시 간 열차가 다른 층에서 도착하고 출발한다. 

In smaller stations, separate platforms should be used and traffic routed so that the two lines of movement do not cross. 
더 작은 역에서는, 두 개의 동선이 서로 교차하지 않도록 별도의 승강장이 사용되어야 하고 교통이 유도되어야 한다. 

In some instances, separate stations are in use. 
어떤 경우에는, 별도의 역이 사용된다. 

Clear and concise direction and routing signs and other means of channelization are desirable.
명확하고 간결한 방향 및 동선 표지와 흐름을 유도하는 다른 수단이 바람직하다.

data.topic_en: Changing Taste Preferences Through Neuroplasticity
data.topic_ko: 신경가소성 덕분에 맛의 선호도는 평생 동안 변화할 수 있다. 어린 시절 형성된 식습관이나 생물학적 차이에도 불구하고, 반복 노출과 변화 의지를 통해 성인도 건강한 음식을 선호하도록 미각을 재조정할 수 있다.
data.expected_title: Rewiring Your Taste: The Brain's Flexibility in Food Preferences
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 과학적 근거 제시 및 실생활 적용 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (정보 전달형 글)
data.passage_html: Despite learned eating behaviors that are formed in early childhood and inborn biological differences, taste preferences can be changed throughout our lives due to neuroplasticity, our brain's amazing adaptability: There is far more flexibility in our food behaviors than most think, even as we age. This is terrific news for adventurous eaters who want to expand their dinner menu — it's a big, tasty world out there! — but it's amazing news for those eager to break poor diet habits. Just as kids gradually learn to like nutritious foods, so, too, can adults readjust their taste. Many who switch from processed grain foods like white bread and white rice to whole grain types, for instance, gradually learn to prefer the nutty flavors and chewy textures. Repeated exposure — and a willingness to change — is the key.
data.topic_sentence_en: Repeated exposure — and a willingness to change — is the key.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 반복된 노출과 변화하려는 의지가 핵심이다. 맛 선호도 변화는 단순히 시간만으로는 부족하며, 지속적인 노출과 적극적인 태도가 필요함을 강조한다.

관계대명사 who: 선행사 Many/those 수식, 주격 관계절로 문장 연결
learn to + 동사원형: '~하는 법을 배우다 / ~하게 되다', 점진적 변화 표현

주제: 반복 노출과 변화 의지를 통한 맛 선호도 변화 가능성 (신경가소성 근거)

data.background: 신경가소성(neuroplasticity)은 뇌가 경험과 학습을 통해 구조와 기능을 변화시킬 수 있는 능력이다. 이는 식습관, 언어 학습, 재활 치료 등 다양한 분야에서 행동 변화의 과학적 근거가 된다.

data.passage:
Despite learned eating behaviors that are formed in early childhood and inborn biological differences,
taste preferences can be changed throughout our lives due to neuroplasticity, our brain’s amazing
adaptability: There is far more flexibility in our food behaviors than most think, even as we age. This is
terrific news for adventurous eaters who want to expand their dinner menu — it’s a big, tasty world out
there! — but it’s amazing news for those eager to break poor diet habits. Just as kids gradually learn to
like nutritious foods, so, too, can adults readjust their taste. Many who switch from processed grain foods
like white bread and white rice to whole grain types, for instance, gradually learn to prefer the nutty
flavors and chewy textures. Repeated exposure — and a willingness to change — is the key.

data.topic_en: Topic: The Role and Motivation of Lighting Cues in Performance
data.topic_ko: 공연 조명은 여러 조명 상태로 구성되며, 각 조명 신호는 배우처럼 계기가 필요하다. 조명 신호는 명백한 변화부터 미묘한 분위기 전환까지 다양하며, 작품의 각 순간마다 조명의 역할을 고려해야 한다.
data.expected_title: Lighting Cues: Motivation and Function in Performance
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 개념 제시 및 예시 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (중립적 설명)
data.passage_html: Most performance lighting is made up of a number of different looks which we have called lighting states. Each lighting cue triggers a change to a new state. Like actors, lighting cues usually need a motivation. This might be something very obvious such as a cue required to brighten a room setting when an actor turns on a light switch or the rapid increase in intensity at the end of a dance number in a traditional musical (known as a button cue). At other times we will need a cue to provide a subtle change in atmosphere over a number of minutes, motivated perhaps by the mention of a sunset or the intention to slowly change the feel of the performance from normal to threatening. The question, 'What will lighting do for this production?' needs to be asked for each moment of the production, each dramatic unit or scene, and each transition.
data.topic_sentence_en: The question, 'What will lighting do for this production?' needs to be asked for each moment of the production, each dramatic unit or scene, and each transition.
data.topic_sentence_ko: '이 작품에서 조명이 무엇을 할 것인가?'라는 질문은 작품의 각 순간, 각 극적 단위 또는 장면, 그리고 각 전환마다 제기될 필요가 있다. 조명의 역할을 지속적으로 고려해야 함을 강조한다.

data.passage:
Most performance lighting is made up of a number of different looks which we have called lighting states. 
대부분의 공연 조명은 우리가 조명 상태라고 부르는 여러 가지 서로 다른 조명 (연출) 장면으로 구성되어 있다. 

Each lighting cue triggers a change to a new state. 
각각의 조명 신호는 새로운 상태로의 변화를 이끌어낸다. 

Like actors, lighting cues usually need a motivation. 
배우처럼, 조명 신호도 보통 계기가 필요하다. 

This might be something very obvious such as a cue required to brighten a room setting when an actor turns on a light switch or the rapid increase in intensity at the end of a dance number in a traditional musical (known as a button cue). 
이는 배우가 전등 스위치를 켤 때 방의 배경을 밝히기 위해 필요한 신호나 전통적인 뮤지컬에서 하나의 댄스 음악이 끝날 때 강도를 빠르게 높이는 것(버튼 큐라고 알려진)처럼 매우 명백한 것일 수도 있다. 

At other times we will need a cue to provide a subtle change in atmosphere over a number of minutes, motivated perhaps by the mention of a sunset or the intention to slowly change the feel of the performance from normal to threatening. 
다른 때에는 몇 분에 걸쳐 분위기의 미묘한 변화를 제공하는 신호가 필요할 것이며, 이는 아마도 일몰에 대한 언급에 의해서나 공연의 분위기를 보통에서 위협적으로 천천히 바꾸고자 하는 의도에서 비롯될 수 있다. 

The question, ‘What will lighting do for this production?’ needs to be asked for each moment of the production, each dramatic unit or scene, and each transition.
 ‘이 작품에서 조명이 무엇을 할 것인가?’라는 질문은, 작품의 각 순간, 각 극적 단위 또는 장면, 그리고 각 전환마다 제기될 필요가 있다.

data.topic_en: Topic: Age-related changes in teenage conformity
data.topic_ko: 청소년기 동조성은 14세 전후로 정점에 이르지만, 이후 성숙하면서 개인의 의견을 지키는 능력이 증가한다. 또래 집단의 영향은 연령대별로 다르게 나타난다.
data.expected_title: The Peak and Decline of Teenage Conformity
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 연구 결과 제시 및 해석
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (객관적 설명)
data.passage_html: Conformity in the teenage years has been studied by putting young people in situations where they are asked to make a choice or decision where that appears to go against what everyone else in the group is saying. The fascinating thing about the results of these studies is that conformity is not spread equally across all age groups. Thus, the willingness to go along with others reaches a peak around the age of 14. After that, this tendency decreases, so that by 16 or 17, young people are much more able to disagree with the group and to stand up for their own opinion. This is an important finding. It demonstrates that the influence of the peer group is not the same across all ages. As young people mature, they become more resilient, and more able to defend their opinions as individuals.

data.passage:
Conformity in the teenage years has been studied by putting young people in situations where they are
asked to make a choice or decision where that appears to go against what everyone else in the group is
saying. The fascinating thing about the results of these studies is that conformity is not spread equally
across all age groups. Thus, the willingness to go along with others reaches a peak around the age of 14.
After that, this tendency decreases, so that by 16 or 17, young people are much more able to disagree
with the group and to stand up for their own opinion. This is an important finding. It demonstrates that
the influence of the peer group is not the same across all ages. As young people mature, they become
more resilient, and more able to defend their opinions as individuals.

data.topic_en: How Rooms Affect Sound Quality
data.topic_ko: 방은 그 안의 소리에 고유한 음향 특성을 부여한다. 헤드폰 소리는 어디서나 동일하지만, 스피커나 마이크로 재생·녹음되는 소리는 방의 구조와 위치에 따라 크게 달라진다.
data.expected_title: The Unique Sound of Every Room
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 원인·결과 중심, 대조 구조
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (객관적 설명)
data.passage_html: Rooms have their own 'sound' because they impose their own characteristics on audio signals contained within them. It's actually kind of remarkable. Sound such as music coming from headphones will sound the same everywhere. No matter what acoustical environment we are in, the headphones sound the same. That's because the room is not part of that playback signal path. But sound such as music from a loudspeaker will sound different in every acoustical environment. Every room where you set up the loudspeaker will cause the sound you hear to be different — sometimes dramatically different; that is because the room is now part of the signal path. Also, in the same room, the loudspeaker will sound different when it is placed in different locations in the room and it will sound different as you move around the room. Similarly, when you are recording a musical instrument, the sound you receive at the microphone will be different in every room and the recorded sound will sound different as the instrument or the microphone is moved.

이 지문은 심경 변화가 없는 객관적 설명문이다.
헤드폰(일정)과 스피커(가변)의 대조를 통해
방의 음향 특성이 소리에 미치는 영향을 설명한다.
grammar_points: 인과 관계: 'because'절을 통한 원인 설명 (①, ③, ⑤)
대조 구조: 'the same' vs. 'different' 반복으로 차이 강조 (②, ④)
양보 표현: 'No matter what'으로 조건 무관성 표현 (②)
cause A to B: '~가 A를 B하게 만들다' 구조 (⑤)
관계사 생략: 'the sound (that) you hear'에서 목적격 관계대명사 생략 (⑤)
시간·조건 부사절: 'when', 'as'를 통한 상황 변화 표현 (⑥)
transformations.title_theme_options:
'방이 만드는 소리의 차이' 또는 '음향 환경의 영향'
주제: 방의 구조가 오디오 신호에 고유한 특성을 부여하여 소리를 변형시킨다는 내용.
대조 구조(헤드폰 vs. 스피커)를 통해 신호 경로의 중요성을 강조.

data.background: 음향학에서 신호 경로(signal path)는 소리가 발생부터 청취까지 거치는 모든 매체를 의미한다. 헤드폰은 귀에 직접 전달되어 방의 영향을 받지 않지만, 스피커는 방의 벽·천장·바닥에서 반사되어 잔향(reverberation)과 정재파(standing wave)를 만들어 소리를 변형시킨다.

data.passage:
Rooms have their own “sound” because they impose their own characteristics on audio signals contained within them. 
방은 자체적인 ‘소리’를 갖는데 이는 그것(방)이 그 안에 담긴 오디오 신호에 자체 특성을 부여하기 때문이다. 

It’s actually kind of remarkable. 
이것은 사실상 꽤 놀랍다. 

Sound such as music coming from headphones will sound the same everywhere. 
헤드폰에서 나오는 음악과 같은 소리는 어디서나 똑같이 들릴 것이다. 

No matter what acoustical environment we are in, the headphones sound the same. 
우리가 어떤 음향 환경에 있든, 헤드폰은 똑같이 들린다. 

That’s because the room is not part of that playback signal path. 
이는 방이 재생 신호 경로의 일부가 아니기 때문이다. 

But sound such as music from a loudspeaker will sound different in every acoustical environment. 
하지만 스피커에서 나오는 음악과 같은 소리는 음향 환경마다 다르게 들린다. 

Every room where you set up the loudspeaker will cause the sound you hear to be different — sometimes dramatically different; that is because the room is now part of the signal path. 
당신이 스피커를 설치하는 모든 방이 당신이 듣는 소리를 모두 다르게 만들 것이며, 때로는 극적으로 달라질 것이다. 이는 그 방이 신호 경로의 일부가 되기 때문이다. 

Also, in the same room, the loudspeaker will sound different when it is placed in different locations in the room and it will sound different as you move around the room. 
또한, 같은 방에서도, 스피커가 그 방의 다른 위치에 놓여있을 때 다르게 들릴 것이며, 당신이 방 안에서 돌아다니면 (또) 다르게 들릴 것이다. 

Similarly, when you are recording a musical instrument, the sound you receive at the microphone will be different in every room and the recorded sound will sound different as the instrument or the microphone is moved.
마찬가지로, 당신이 악기를 녹음하고 있을 때, 마이크로 수신되는 소리는 방마다 달라질 것이며, 악기나 마이크의 위치가 옮겨지면 녹음된 소리는 다르게 들릴 것이다.

data.topic_en: Topic: Using habit bundling to build desired behaviors
data.topic_ko: 습관은 생존에 필수적이며, 나쁜 것이 아니다. 습관을 깨기보다는 원하는 습관(운동)을 유혹적인 습관(오디오북 듣기)과 결합하는 '유혹 번들링' 전략이 효과적이다. 실험 결과 기기를 체육관에서만 사용하게 한 참가자들의 방문율이 51% 증가했고, 실험 후에도 2/3가 유료로 이를 유지했다.
data.expected_title: Habit Bundling: Combining Temptation with Desired Behavior
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 연구 사례 중심 / 전략 제시
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (설명문)
data.passage_html: Habits aren't bad. We need them to survive. Understanding how the brain uses habits, and how you can work with them, is essential for business. One question to ask is, are you really trying to break a habit, or would you do better by attaching to another one (known as temptation bundling)? Wharton professor Katy Milkman led a research project called Holding the Hunger Games Hostage at the Gym, where participants could only have access to their audio devices while at the gym. This uses a tempting habit — listening to that awesome audiobook — and combines it with a habit people would like to build, but may otherwise feel forced to put off, like exercising. The participants whose devices were 'held hostage' were 51 percent more likely to visit the gym. And the really amazing thing is what happened after it was over: nearly two­thirds opted to pay to have gym­only access for their devices!

data.passage:
Habits aren’t bad. We need them to survive. Understanding how the brain uses habits, and how you can
work with them, is essential for business. One question to ask is, are you really trying to break a habit, or
would you do better by attaching to another one (known as temptation bundling)? Wharton professor
Katy Milkman led a research project called Holding the Hunger Games Hostage at the Gym, where
participants could only have access to their audio devices while at the gym. This uses a tempting habit —
listening to that awesome audiobook — and combines it with a habit people would like to build, but may
otherwise feel forced to put off, like exercising. The participants whose devices were “held hostage” were
51 percent more likely to visit the gym. And the really amazing thing is what happened after it was over:
nearly twothirds opted to pay to have gymonly access for their devices!

data.topic_en: Topic: Gary Graffman's musical journey and his dedication to piano despite adversity
data.topic_ko: 피아니스트 Gary Graffman의 생애와 음악에 대한 헌신을 다룬 글이다. 그는 오른손 손가락의 통제력을 잃었지만 왼손만으로 연주하는 작품에 집중하며 연주를 포기하지 않았고, 이후 교육자로서 많은 젊은 음악가들을 가르치며 음악에 대한 평생의 헌신을 이어갔다.
data.expected_title: A Pianist's Lifelong Dedication to Music
data.text_type: 전기문 (biography) — 시간 순서에 따른 인물의 업적과 극복 과정 중심
data.mood_change: 성공·명성 (won the Leventritt Award, played concerts worldwide) → 좌절·위기 (lost control of the fingers) → 재도전·헌신 (never gave up, focused on works for the left hand)
data.passage_html: Gary Graffman was born in New York City in 1928 and began playing the piano at the age of three. When he was seven, he entered the Curtis Institute of Music, where he received training that laid the foundation for his career. In 1949, he won the Leventritt Award and then played concerts worldwide. In the late 1970s, Graffman lost control of the fingers on his right hand, but he never gave up playing the piano. Reconsidering the traditional piano performance convention of using both hands, he focused on works for the left hand alone, such as Ravel's Piano Concerto for the Left Hand. Graffman returned to the Curtis Institute of Music in 1980 as a member of the piano faculty. There he taught many outstanding young musicians, including Lang Lang, and his lifelong commitment to music continues to inspire people today.
data.topic_sentence_en: his lifelong commitment to music continues to inspire people today
data.topic_sentence_ko: 음악에 대한 그의 평생의 헌신은 오늘날에도 사람들에게 계속 영감을 준다 — 역경 속에서도 포기하지 않고 새로운 방식으로 음악을 이어간 Graffman의 삶 자체가 헌신과 극복의 메시지를 전달한다는 의미

data.background: Gary Graffman(1928-)은 미국의 세계적인 피아니스트이자 음악 교육자이다. Curtis Institute of Music에서 수학하고 Leventritt Award(당대 최고 권위의 클래식 음악상)를 수상하며 명성을 얻었으나, 1970년대 후반 오른손에 focal dystonia(국소 근긴장이상증)가 발병해 연주 경력에 위기를 맞았다. 이후 왼손만을 위한 레퍼토리로 전환했고, 교육자로서 Lang Lang 등 세계적 피아니스트를 배출했다.

Gary Graffman was born in New York City in 1928 and began playing the piano at the age of three. When

data.topic_en: The Development of the Modern Piano Factory System
data.topic_ko: 산업혁명은 단순히 새로운 기계를 발명한 것이 아니라 현대적 공장 시스템이라는 새로운 제조 과정을 창조했다. 피아노 제조업은 견습생 제도에서 분업화된 공장 시스템으로 전환되며 효율성과 품질이 크게 향상되었다.
data.expected_title: The Birth of the Modern Piano Factory
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 시간 순서에 따른 제조 방식의 변화
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (설명문)
data.passage_html: The Industrial Revolution did more than just invent new materials or machines. It invented a new process for manufacturing: the modern factory. Prior to around 1850, most piano shops used an apprentice system in which a master builder taught younger workers the skills of the trade, usually over the course of many years. The larger piano shops did have specialists — for example, one person who specialized in soundboards, another who made hammers, and so on. But even the largest piano makers of that time produced no more than 5-10 pianos per year per worker, as pianos were still made essentially one at a time. The development of a true piano factory occurred first in the United States, led by Jonas Chickering in Boston and later the Steinway family in New York City. These factories led to improvements in both efficiency and quality. For example, one of the factories of the time had its own iron casting facility where it made its metal plates, which were thought to be the best available.
data.topic_sentence_en: The Industrial Revolution invented a new process for manufacturing: the modern factory.

data.passage:
The Industrial Revolution did more than just invent new materials or machines. 
산업 혁명은 단순히 새로운 물건과 기계를 만들어 내는 것 이상의 일을 했다. 

It invented a new process for manufacturing: the modern factory. 
그것은 제조의 새로운 ‘과정’, 즉 현대적인 공장을 창조했다. 

Prior to around 1850, most piano shops used an apprentice system in which a master builder taught younger workers the skills of the trade, usually over the course of many years. 
1850년 즈음 이전에, 대부분의 피아노 가게들은 한 명의 숙련가가 젊은 직원에게 업계의 기술을 가르치는 견습생 제도를 이용했는데, 보통 수년이 걸렸다. 

The larger piano shops did have specialists — for example, one person who specialized in soundboards, another who made hammers, and so on. 
더 큰 피아노 가게들에는 전문가가 있었는데, 예를 들어 소리판을 전문적으로 만드는 사람, (피아노의) 해머를 만드는 사람 등이 있었다. 

But even the largest piano makers of that time produced no more than 5-10 pianos per year per worker, as pianos were still made essentially one at a time. 
하지만 그 당시의 가장 큰 피아노 제조사도 기본적으로 직원 한 사람당 연간 5~10대 이상의 피아노를 만들어내지 못했는데, (이는) 피아노가 여전히 한 번에 한 대만 만들어졌기 때문이었다. 

The development of a true piano factory occurred first in the United States, led by Jonas Chickering in Boston and later the Steinway family in New York City. These factories led to improvements in both efficiency and quality. 
진정한 피아노 공장의 발전은 미국에서 먼저 일어났는데, 보스턴의 Jonas Chickering과 이후 뉴욕시의 Steinway 가문에 의해 주도되었다. 이런 공장들은 효율성과 품질 모두의 향상으로 이어졌다. 

For example, one of the factories of the time had its own iron casting facility where it made its metal plates, which were thought to be the best available.
예를 들어, 그 당시의 한 공장은 금속판을 만들 수 있는 자체 철 주조 시설을 가지고 있었고, 그것은 가장 유용하다고 여겨졌다.

data.topic_en: Topic: The Nature and Utility of Conceptual Models
data.topic_ko: 개념 모델은 실제와 다르더라도 유용하다면 효과적인 도구가 될 수 있다. 컴퓨터의 폴더나 클라우드 이미지처럼 단순화된 개념이 사용자의 이해를 돕지만, 때로는 혼란을 야기하기도 한다.

data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (중립적 설명)
data.passage_html: A conceptual model is an explanation, usually highly simplified, of how something works. It doesn't have to be complete or even accurate as long as it is useful. The files, folders, and icons you see displayed on a computer screen help people create the conceptual model of documents and folders inside the computer, or of apps or applications on the screen. In fact, there are no folders inside the computer — those are effective conceptualizations designed to make them easier to use. Sometimes these depictions can add to the confusion, however. When reading email or visiting a website, the material appears to be on the device, for that is where it is displayed and used. But in fact, in many cases the actual material is 'in the cloud,' located on some distant machine. The conceptual model is of one, coherent image, whereas it may actually consist of parts, each located on different machines that could be almost anywhere in the world.

A conceptual model is an explanation, usually highly simplified, of how something works. It doesn’t have

data.topic_en: Limitations of Thesis-Based Doctoral Programmes and Structured Alternatives
data.topic_ko: 전통적인 논문 기반 박사 과정은 깊이 있는 연구를 가능하게 하지만 좁은 초점으로 인해 폭넓은 기술 개발을 제한할 수 있다. 유럽의 일부 대학들은 수업과 전이 가능한 기술 훈련을 결합한 구조화된 접근을 도입하여 학생들이 다양한 진로에 대비하도록 돕고 있다.
data.expected_title: Rethinking Doctoral Education: From Narrow Specialization to Broad Skill Development
data.text_type: 논증문 (argumentative) — 문제 제기 및 해결책 제시
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (객관적 논증)
data.passage_html: In a thesis‑based doctoral programme, students typically spend a significant amount of time and effort researching a specific topic. While this deep dive into a particular area allows for thorough exploration and understanding, it can also result in narrowing the focus. As students become deeply absorbed in their research, they may spend less time exploring related fields or acquiring skills outside their immediate area of study. Consequently, this singular focus may limit the breadth of knowledge and skills developed during the programme, potentially hindering students' ability to adapt to diverse career paths or address interdisciplinary challenges. Some universities in Europe have recognised the limitations of traditional thesis‑based doctoral programmes and have started to implement more structured approaches. These structured programmes often combine research with coursework and training in transferable skills. By incorporating coursework, seminars, workshops and internships into the curriculum, they aim to provide students with a broader skill set and better prepare them for a variety of career paths beyond academia.
data.topic_sentence_en: Some universities in Europe have recognised the limitations of traditional thesis‑based doctoral programmes and have started to implement more structured approaches.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 유럽의 일부 대학들은 전통적인 논문 기반 박사 과정의 한계를 인식하게 되어 더 구조화된 접근을 시행하기 시작했다. 이는 문제 인식 후 해결책 제시로의 전환을 나타내는 중심 문장이다.

data.passage:
In a thesis‑based doctoral programme, students typically spend a significant amount of time and effort researching a specific topic. 
논문 기반 박사 과정에서는, 학생들은 일반적으로 특정 주제를 연구하는 데 상당한 시간과 노력을 들인다. 

While this deep dive into a particular area allows for thorough exploration and understanding, it can also result in narrowing the focus. 
특정 분야에 이처럼 깊이 몰두하는 것은 철저한 탐구와 이해를 가능하게 하지만, 이것은 또한 초점을 좁히는 결과를 가져올 수도 있다. 

As students become deeply absorbed in their research, they may spend less time exploring related fields or acquiring skills outside their immediate area of study. 
학생들이 자신의 연구에 깊이 몰두함에 따라, 그들은 관련 분야를 탐색하거나 자신들의 직접적인 연구 분야 밖의 기술을 습득하는 데는 더 적은 시간을 보내게 될 수도 있다. 

Consequently, this singular focus may limit the breadth of knowledge and skills developed during the programme, potentially hindering students’ ability to adapt to diverse career paths or address interdisciplinary challenges. 
결과적으로, 이러한 단일한 초점은 (박사) 과정 동안 개발되는 지식과 기술의 폭을 제한하여, 잠재적으로 학생들이 다양한 진로에 적응하거나 학제 간 도전 과제를 처리하는 능력을 저해할 수 있다. 

Some universities in Europe have recognised the limitations of traditional thesis‑based doctoral programmes and have started to implement more structured approaches. 
유럽의 일부 대학들은 전통적인 논문 기반 박사 과정의 한계를 인식하게 되어 더 구조화된 접근을 시행하기 시작했다. 

These structured programmes often combine research with coursework and training in transferable skills. 
이러한 구조화된 (박사) 과정들은 흔히 연구와 전이 가능한 기술에 대한 수업 및 훈련을 결합한다. 

By incorporating coursework, seminars, workshops and internships into the curriculum, they aim to provide students with a broader skill set and better prepare them for a variety of career paths beyond academia.
수업, 세미나, 워크숍, 인턴십을 교육 과정에 통합함으로써, 이 (과정)들은 학생들에게 더 폭넓은 기술 세트를 제공하고 학계를 넘어 다양한 진로에 더 잘 대비시키는 것을 목표로 한다.

data.topic_en: Topic: Walking - Burden in Developing World vs. Health in Developed World
data.topic_ko: 개발도상국에서는 걷기가 생존을 위한 불가피한 부담이지만, 북미와 서유럽에서는 효율적이고 건강한 활동으로 여겨진다. 아이러니하게도 선진국에서는 길거리에서 할 수 있는 걷기를 헬스클럽의 러닝머신으로 대체하고 있다.
data.expected_title: The Paradox of Walking: Burden vs. Benefit
data.text_type: 대조·논설문 (expository/contrast) — 개발도상국과 선진국의 걷기에 대한 태도 비교 중심
data.mood_change: 대조적 관점 (can hardly spare energy → healthful and natural → ironically simulate walking)
data.passage_html: The principal transportation mode in the developing world, even in large cities, is still walking because of constraints on the resources needed to operate extensive transit systems. People cover long distances on foot every day and expend human energy that they can hardly spare. Walking under those conditions is an unavoidable burden that consumes productive capability. In North America and Western Europe, however, the attitude and policies are just the opposite: walking is efficient, healthful, and natural. We should do more of it — almost everybody agrees — and some of the current trends should be reversed. Ironically, among the most popular exercise machines in health clubs and in homes are treadmills that simulate walking, which could be otherwise accomplished with a transport purpose on the street.
data.topic_sentence_en: In North America and Western Europe, however, the attitude and policies are just the opposite: walking is efficient, healthful, and natural.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 북미와 서유럽에서는 태도와 정책이 정반대로, 걷기는 효율적이고 건강하며 자연스러운 것이다. — 선진국과 개발도상국의 걷기에 대한 근본적 인식 차이를 드러내는 핵심 문장.

blank_inference: 개발도상국에서는 자원 제약으로 걷기가 '불가피한 부담'이지만, 선진국에서는 '건강한 활동'으로 인식된다는 대조

선진국: 효율·건강·자연 → 더 해야 함 (efficient, healthful, should do more)
아이러니: 길거리 대신 러닝머신 (ironically, simulate walking)
★ 같은 행위(걷기)가 맥락에 따라 정반대 의미를 가지는 역설적 구조

수동태: 'should be reversed' (현재의 추세가 역전되어야 한다)
도치 구문: 'among... are treadmills' (전치사구 강조)
콜론(:) 동격 설명: 'the opposite: walking is efficient' (구체적 내용 제시)

data.passage:
The principal transportation mode in the developing world, even in large cities, is still walking because of
constraints on the resources needed to operate extensive transit systems. People cover long distances on
foot every day and expend human energy that they can hardly spare. Walking under those conditions is
an unavoidable burden that consumes productive capability. In North America and Western Europe,
however, the attitude and policies are just the opposite: walking is efficient, healthful, and natural. We
should do more of it — almost everybody agrees — and some of the current trends should be reversed.
Ironically, among the most popular exercise machines in health clubs and in homes are treadmills that
simulate walking, which could be otherwise accomplished with a transport purpose on the street.

data.topic_en: Topic: Children's Understanding of Imagination versus Reality and the Role of Emotion
data.topic_ko: 아이들은 세 살이 되면 상상의 사물이 실재하지 않음을 이해한다. 그러나 감정, 특히 두려움은 이러한 인지적 이해를 방해하여 상상 속 괴물에 대한 회피 행동을 유발할 수 있다.
data.expected_title: Children's Understanding of Imagination and Reality: The Disruptive Role of Emotion
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 연구 결과 제시 및 사례 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (인지적 이해 vs 정서적 반응의 대비)
data.passage_html: Research shows that, by age three, children understand that imaginary objects do not come to life. This is especially clear with respect to everyday objects — children know that even though they imagine a pencil in an empty box, the box will remain empty. However, emotion can sometimes disrupt this understanding, or at least its expression. That is, even though a child knows that monsters are not real, the thought of a monster under a bed might be enough to make a child refuse to go into his room at night. Indeed, research shows that children have a more difficult time displaying their understanding of the causal relations between imagination and reality when they are asked to pretend or imagine scary things, like monsters. In one study, preschool children were shown an empty box and were asked to imagine a monster inside. All children agreed that the box was empty. However, when they were left alone with the box they exhibited fear and avoidance of it.
data.topic_sentence_en: However, emotion can sometimes disrupt this understanding, or at least its expression.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 그러나, 감정은 때때로 이러한 이해를, 혹은 적어도 그 표현을 방해할 수 있다. — 아이들이 인지적으로는 상상과 현실을 구분하지만, 감정(특히 두려움)이 개입하면 이 이해가 행동으로 표현되지 않을 수 있음을 나타내는 핵심 문장.

data.passage:
Research shows that, by age three, children understand that imaginary objects do not come to life. 
연구에서는 아이들이 세 살이 되면, 상상의 사물이 살아나 움직이지 않음을 이해한다는 것을 보여준다. 

This is especially clear with respect to everyday objects — children know that even though they imagine a pencil in an empty box, the box will remain empty. 
이는 일상적인 사물에 관해 특히 명확하다. 아이들은 빈 상자 안의 연필을 상상하더라도, 상자는 여전히 비어 있을 것임을 알고 있다. 

However, emotion can sometimes disrupt this understanding, or at least its expression. 
그러나, 감정은 때때로 이러한 이해를, 혹은 적어도 그 표현을 방해할 수 있다. 

That is, even though a child knows that monsters are not real, the thought of a monster under a bed might be enough to make a child refuse to go into his room at night. 
즉, 비록 아이가 괴물이 실제가 아니라는 것을 안다고 하더라도, 침대 밑에 괴물이 있을 거라는 생각만으로 아이가 밤에 자기 방에 들어가기를 거부하도록 만들기에 충분할 수 있다. 

Indeed, research shows that children have a more difficult time displaying their understanding of the causal relations between imagination and reality when they are asked to pretend or imagine scary things, like monsters. 
실제로, 아이들이 괴물과 같이 무서운 것을 가장하거나 상상하도록 요청받을 때 상상과 현실 간의 인과 관계에 대한 이해를 드러내는 데 더 어려움을 겪음을 연구에서 보여준다. 

In one study, preschool children were shown an empty box and were asked to imagine a monster inside. 
한 연구에서, 유치원생들이 빈 상자를 보고 그 안의 괴물을 상상해 보라는 요청을 받았다. 

All children agreed that the box was empty. 
모든 아이들은 그 상자가 비어 있다는 데 동의했다. 

However, when they were left alone with the box they exhibited fear and avoidance of it.
그러나, 그들이 상자와 함께 혼자 남겨졌을 때 그들은 그것(상자)에 대한 두려움과 회피를 보였다.

data.topic_en: Topic: The importance of decluttering play spaces for children
data.topic_ko: 아이들의 놀이 공간에 물건이 너무 많으면 선택 장애와 압도감을 느낄 수 있다. 공간을 정리하면 아이들이 자신이 가진 것을 명확히 인식하고 상상력을 발휘할 수 있다.
data.expected_title: Less is More: Decluttering Play Spaces for Kids
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 문제 제시 및 해결 방안 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (논리적 설명 중심)
data.passage_html: Just like how other rooms in your home can cause anxiety when filled with too much stuff, the same is true for kids. If the play space houses every single toy that has ever been purchased for them since birth, they may not be able to express their feelings, but they can feel overwhelmed by so much stuff. This reminds us of how women look in their closets packed full of clothes and think, I have nothing to wear. Revision helps everyone see what they have and use what they own. When there's too much to see, too much to step over, and too much input, kids have a hard time making a choice. Streamlining a play space is so important. You want your kids to feel inspired and imaginative in the room — not overcome with indecision.
data.topic_sentence_en: Revision helps everyone see what they have and use what they own.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 정리는 모든 사람이 자신이 가진 것을 파악하고 소유한 것을 활용하도록 돕는다. 핵심 해결책을 제시하는 중심 문장.

data.passage:
Just like how other rooms in your home can cause anxiety when filled with too much stuff, the same is
true for kids. If the play space houses every single toy that has ever been purchased for them since birth,
they may not be able to express their feelings, but they can feel overwhelmed by so much stuff. This
reminds us of how women look in their closets packed full of clothes and think, I have nothing to wear.
Revision helps everyone see what they have and use what they own. When there’s too much to see, too
much to step over, and too much input, kids have a hard time making a choice. Streamlining a play space
is soimportant. You want your kids to feel inspired and imaginative in the room — not overcome with
indecision.

data.topic_en: Topic: Transforming Familiarity into Mastery Through Teaching
data.topic_ko: 학생들은 익숙함을 진정한 숙달로 착각하는 '능력의 착각'에 빠지기 쉽다. 타인을 가르치는 행위는 깊은 처리와 능동적 인출을 요구하며, 지식의 공백을 드러내고 표면적 친숙함을 진정한 숙달로 전환시킨다.
data.expected_title: The Illusion of Competence: Teaching as the Path to True Mastery
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 문제 제시 → 원인 분석 → 해결책 제시 구조
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (설명문)
data.passage_html: Students often mistake familiarity with true mastery, creating a dangerous 'illusion of competence' where recognizing information feels like genuine knowledge, but they struggle when asked to recall or apply it independently. This cognitive bias, strengthened by passive study methods, leads learners to overestimate their understanding. Teaching materials (even informally or imaginatively) actively counters this illusion by requiring deep processing, active recall, structured organization, and revealing gaps in knowledge. It introduces powerful methods like teaching imaginary students, peer teaching in study groups, employing the Feynman Technique, and writing explanations for others. Ultimately, adopting the teacher mindset transforms surface familiarity into real mastery, exposing and filling gaps in knowledge and ensuring solid, reliable understanding.
data.topic_sentence_en: Ultimately, adopting the teacher mindset transforms surface familiarity into real mastery, exposing and filling gaps in knowledge and ensuring solid, reliable understanding.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 궁극적으로 가르치는 사람의 사고방식을 채택하는 것은 표면적 친숙함을 진정한 숙달로 전환시키며, 지식의 공백을 드러내고 채워서 견고하고 신뢰할 수 있는 이해를 보장한다.

data.background: 인지심리학에서 '능력의 착각(illusion of competence)'은 학습자가 정보를 인식할 수 있다는 것만으로 그것을 실제로 이해하고 있다고 착각하는 현상이다. 파인만 기법(Feynman Technique)은 노벨 물리학상 수상자 리처드 파인만이 개발한 학습법으로, 복잡한 개념을 단순하게 설명함으로써 진정한 이해를 확인하는 방법이다.

data.passage:
Students often mistake familiarity with true mastery, creating a dangerous “illusion of competence” where
recognizing information feels like genuine knowledge, but they struggle when asked to recall or apply it
independently. This cognitive bias, strengthened by passive study methods, leads learners to overestimate
their understanding. Teaching materials (even informally or imaginatively) actively counters this illusion by
requiring deep processing, active recall, structured organization, and revealing gaps in knowledge. It
introduces powerful methods like teaching imaginary students, peerteaching in study groups, employing
the Feynman Technique, and writing explanations for others. Ultimately, adopting the teacher mindset
transforms surface familiarity into real mastery, exposing and filling gaps in knowledge and ensuring
solid, reliable understanding.

data.topic_en: The aesthetics of everyday life and its philosophical recognition
data.topic_ko: 일상생활의 미학은 미적 경험의 해방과 확장에서 나온 대표적 발전이다. 듀이는 미적 경험을 예술과 삶의 분리가 아닌 인간 삶의 생물학적·문화적 조건에 기반한 자아와 세계의 상호침투로 보았다.
data.expected_title: The Aesthetics of Everyday Life: From Dewey's Vision to Contemporary Recognition
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 개념 소개 및 철학적 논증 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (학술적 논증)
data.passage_html: Perhaps the best‑known development to emerge from the liberation and expansion of aesthetic experience is the aesthetics of everyday life. Although there is presently a flowering of work on everyday aesthetics, the possibility of aesthetic gratification in ordinary objects and events has long been recognized, even if degraded and dismissed by prevalent philosophical theory. Widely valued by poets, especially Romantic poets and those in Asian traditions, the aesthetic in everyday situations has also been recognized by novelists. It may be most convenient, though, to locate its contemporary intellectual origins in John Dewey's Art as Experience. In that book Dewey argued against the separation of art from life by basing aesthetic experience on the biological and cultural conditions of human life. He located the aesthetic, not in an internalized awareness of sensation and feeling but in 'a complete interpenetration of self and the world of objects and events.' Further, Dewey maintained that 'the aesthetic is the clarified and intensified development of traits that belong to every normally complete experience.'
data.topic_sentence_en: He located the aesthetic, not in an internalized awareness of sensation and feeling but in 'a complete interpenetration of self and the world of objects and events.'
data.topic_sentence_ko: 듀이는 미적인 것을 감각과 감정의 내면화된 인식이 아니라 '자아와 사물 및 사건 세계의 완전한 상호침투'에서 찾았다. 이는 예술을 삶과 분리된 특권적 영역이 아닌 일상 경험의 연장으로 본 혁신적 관점이다.

data.passage:
Perhaps the best‑known development to emerge from the liberation and expansion of aesthetic experience is the aesthetics of everyday life. 
아마도 미적 경험의 해방과 확장으로부터 나타난 가장 잘 알려진 발전은 일상생활의 미학일 것이다. 

Although there is presently a flowering of work on everyday aesthetics, the possibility of aesthetic gratification in ordinary objects and events has long been recognized, even if degraded and dismissed by prevalent philosophical theory. 
현재 일상 미학에 관한 활발한 연구가 있지만, 평범한 사물과 사건 속의 미적 만족감에 대한 가능성은 오래전부터 인식되어 왔는데, 비록 지배적인 철학 이론에 의해 격하되고 무시 받아 왔음에도 (그러했다). 

Widely valued by poets, especially Romantic poets and those in Asian traditions, the aesthetic in everyday situations has also been recognized by novelists. 
일상적 상황 속의 미적인 것들은 시인들, 특히 낭만주의 시인이나 아시아 전통에 속한 시인들에 의해 널리 가치 있게 여겨졌고, 소설가들에 의해서도 인식되어 왔다. 

It may be most convenient, though, to locate its contemporary intellectual origins in John Dewey’s Art as Experience. 
그럼에도 불구하고, 그 현대적 지적 기원을 John Dewey의 ‘Art as Experience’에서 찾는 것이 가장 적절할 것이다. 

In that book Dewey argued against the separation of art from life by basing aesthetic experience on the biological and cultural conditions of human life. 
그 책에서 Dewey는 미적 경험을 인간 삶의 생물학적, 문화적 조건에 기반하여 예술과 삶의 분리에 맞서는 주장을 펼쳤다. 

He located the aesthetic, not in an internalized awareness of sensation and feeling but in “a complete interpenetration of self and the world of objects and events.” 
그는 미적인 것을 감각과 감정의 내면화된 인식이 아니라 “자아와 사물 및 사건의 세계의 완전한 상호 침투”에서 찾았다. 

Further, Dewey maintained that “the aesthetic is the clarified and intensified development of traits that belong to every normally complete experience.”
더 나아가, 듀이는 “미적인 것이란 모든 일상적으로 완전한 경험에 속하는 특성들이 명확해지고 강화되어 발전된 형태이다.”라는 입장을 유지했다.

data.topic_en: Topic: The Invalidity of Claiming Human Moral Superiority Over Non-Moral Agents
data.topic_ko: 인간이 도덕적 행위자의 능력을 가졌다는 이유만으로 동물이나 식물보다 도덕적으로 우월하다고 주장할 수 없다. 도덕적 우월성 판단은 동일하게 도덕적 기준이 적용될 수 있는 도덕적 행위자들 사이에서만 유의미하기 때문이다.
data.expected_title: Human Moral Superiority: A Logical Fallacy
data.text_type: 논증문 (argumentative) — 논리적 반박과 근거 제시 중심
data.mood_change: 해당 없음 (논리적 논증)
data.passage_html: One cannot validly argue that humans are morally superior beings on the ground that they possess, while others lack, the capacities of a moral agent. The reason is that, as far as moral standards are concerned, only beings that have the capacities of a moral agent can meaningfully be said to be either morally good or morally bad. Only moral agents can be judged to be morally better or worse than others, and the others in question must be moral agents themselves. Judgments of moral superiority are based on the comparative merits or deficiencies of the entities being judged, and these merits and deficiencies are all moral ones, that is, ones determined by moral standards. One entity is correctly judged morally superior to another if it is the case that, when valid moral standards are applied to both entities, the first fulfills them to a greater degree than the second. Both entities, therefore, must fall within the range of application of moral standards. This would not be the case, however, if humans were being judged superior to animals and plants, since the latter are not moral agents.
data.topic_sentence_en: One cannot validly argue that humans are morally superior beings on the ground that they possess, while others lack, the capacities of a moral agent.
data.topic_sentence_ko: 인간이 도덕적 행위자의 능력을 지니고 있는 반면, 다른 존재들은 그러한 능력이 부족하다는 근거로 인간이 도덕적으로 우월하다고 타당하게 주장할 수 없다. 이는 전체 논증의 핵심 주장이다.

data.passage:
One cannot validly argue that humans are morally superior beings on the ground that they possess, while others lack, the capacities of a moral agent. 
인간이 도덕적 행위자의 능력을 지니고 있는 반면, 다른 존재들은 (그러한 능력이) 부족하다는 근거로 인간이 도덕적으로 우월한 존재라고 타당하게 주장할 수 없다. 

The reason is that, as far as moral standards are concerned, only beings that have the capacities of a moral agent can meaningfully be said to be either morally good or morally bad. 
그 이유는, 도덕적 기준에 관해서는, 도덕적 행위자의 능력을 지닌 존재만이 유의미하게 도덕적으로 선하‘거나’ 도덕적으로 악하‘다고’ 말할 수 있기 때문이다. 

Only moral agents can be judged to be morally better or worse than others, and the others in question must be moral agents themselves. 
오직 도덕적 행위자만이 다른 존재들보다 도덕적으로 더 낫거나 더 나쁘다고 판단될 수 있으며, (비교의) 대상이 되는 다른 존재들도 그 자체로 도덕적 행위자여야 한다. 

Judgments of moral superiority are based on the comparative merits or deficiencies of the entities being judged, and these merits and deficiencies are all moral ones, that is, ones determined by moral standards. 
도덕적 우월성에 대한 판단은 (도덕적) 판단 대상이 되는 존재들의 상대적인 장점이나 결함에 근거하며, 이러한 장점과 결함은 모두 도덕적인 것들, 즉, 도덕적 기준에 의해 결정되는 것들이다. 

One entity is correctly judged morally superior to another if it is the case that, when valid moral standards are applied to both entities, the first fulfills them to a greater degree than the second. 
타당한 도덕적 기준을 두 존재 모두에게 적용했을 때, 첫 번째 존재가 두 번째 존재보다 그것(기준)들을 더 높은 정도로 충족하는 경우에만, 앞의 존재가 또 다른 존재보다 도덕적으로 우월하다고 정확하게 판단된다. 

Both entities, therefore, must fall within the range of application of moral standards. 
따라서, 두 존재 모두 도덕적 기준의 적용 범위 안에 속해야 한다. 

This would not be the case, however, if humans were being judged superior to animals and plants, since the latter are not moral agents. 
그러나, 인간이 동물이나 식물보다 우월하다고 판단되는 경우에는 이것이 성립되지 않는데, 왜냐하면 후자들은 도덕적 행위자가 아니기 때문이다.

data.topic_en: Topic: The Development of Abstract Numbers in Ancient Sumeria
data.topic_ko: 기원전 3000년경 수메르인들은 숫자를 대상으로부터 분리하여 추상적 개념으로 발전시켰다. 이는 현대 사고의 기반이 된 지적 도약이었다.
data.expected_title: The Emancipation of Numbers: How Sumerians Created Abstract Numerical Concepts
data.text_type: 설명문 (expository) — 역사적 발전 과정 중심 / 개념의 진화 서술
data.mood_change: 구체적 기록 (pictures of objects) → 추상적 사고 (emancipated number)
data.passage_html: From around 3000 BC, the Sumerian officials would mark their lists of goods on clay tablets. If they wanted to record five fish, they would mark five pictures of a fish. Their first great intellectual leap came from separating the number from the object they were counting. In other words, they would represent five fish with a numeral for the number five alongside a symbol for the fish. If they wanted to describe five of something else, they realized they could keep the same numeral and trade the object symbol for a cow, or a jar of oil, or whatever else they were interested in. The Sumerians had developed the idea of an emancipated number, existing in its own right and independent of whatever it is being used to count. It is easy to take the emancipated number for granted as it is so deeply set into modern thought, but to the earliest civilizations it was intellectually new and extremely powerful.
data.topic_sentence_en: The Sumerians had developed the idea of an emancipated number, existing in its own right and independent of whatever it is being used to count.
